stem: The symmetric matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[b]] & [[a]] \end{pmatrix} \) has two distinct eigenvalues, so the shortcut theorem (n distinct eigenvalues \( \Rightarrow \) diagonalizable) guarantees \( A \) is diagonalizable. What is the largest eigenvalue of \( A \)?
solution: The eigenvalues of \( A \) are \( a+b \) and \( a-b \); since \( [[b]]>0 \) they are distinct, confirming the shortcut applies. Here they are \( [[a]]+[[b]] \) and \( [[a]]-[[b]] \); the larger is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the smaller eigenvalue \( a-b \), not the largest.
This is the shared diagonal entry (the average of the two eigenvalues), not the largest eigenvalue.
This doubles the diagonal entry rather than adding the correct offset \( b \).
This is the off-diagonal entry, not an eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct largest eigenvalue.
This is two more than the correct largest eigenvalue.

stem: The triangular matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ 0 & [[d]] & [[e]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[f]] \end{pmatrix} \) has 3 distinct eigenvalues, so the shortcut theorem guarantees it is diagonalizable. What is the sum of its eigenvalues?
solution: The (distinct) eigenvalues of this triangular matrix are its diagonal entries \( [[a]], [[d]], [[f]] \), so their sum is \( [[a]]+[[d]]+[[f]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the PRODUCT of the eigenvalues, not their sum.
This sums the off-diagonal entries instead of the diagonal (eigenvalue) entries.
This omits the third eigenvalue \( [[f]] \) from the sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by two from the correct sum.

stem: The triangular matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ 0 & [[d]] & [[e]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[f]] \end{pmatrix} \) has 3 distinct (nonzero) eigenvalues, so the shortcut theorem guarantees it is diagonalizable, and \( A \) is invertible. What is the product of its eigenvalues?
solution: The eigenvalues are the diagonal entries \( [[a]], [[d]], [[f]] \), so their product is \( ([[a]])([[d]])([[f]]) = [[answer]] \) (this equals \( \det(A) \), confirming \( A \) is invertible).
distractor_rationales: This is the SUM of the eigenvalues, not their product.
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This multiplies entries from the first row instead of the diagonal.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: The triangular matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ 0 & [[d]] & [[e]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[f]] \end{pmatrix} \) has 3 distinct eigenvalues. By the shortcut theorem, how many linearly independent eigenvectors is \( A \) guaranteed to have (i.e. what is the dimension of the eigenvector basis the theorem provides)?
solution: The shortcut theorem states that 3 DISTINCT eigenvalues guarantee 3 linearly independent eigenvectors (one per eigenvalue), exactly enough to diagonalize this \( 3\times3 \) matrix. So the guaranteed count is [[answer]].
distractor_rationales: This under-counts by one; each of the 3 distinct eigenvalues contributes its own independent eigenvector.
The theorem cannot guarantee MORE independent eigenvectors than the matrix's size, 3.
This assumes only one eigenvector total, ignoring that every distinct eigenvalue contributes one.
This confuses the eigenvector count with the total number of matrix entries.
A diagonalizable matrix with distinct eigenvalues has a nontrivial eigenbasis, not zero eigenvectors.
